skill.name: code-refactor-for-reproducibility
skill.description: Use when refactoring research code for publication, adding documentation to existing analysis scripts, creating reproducible computational workflows, or preparing code for sharing with collaborators. Transforms research code into publication-ready, reproducible workflows. Adds documentation, implements error handling, creates environment specifications, and ensures computational reproducibility for scientific publications.
skill.summary: Use when refactoring research code for publication, adding documentation to existing analysis script...
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 123 potential issues but after AI evaluation, all findings are false positives or legitimate functionality for a code generation tool. The skill uses subprocess to capture pip dependencies for reproducibility documentation, writes generated project files to user-specified output directories, and sets PYTHONHASHSEED for deterministic builds. No credential exfiltration, malicious network calls, or command injection vectors were found. Heuristic combination findings (code execution + network + credential access) are dismissed as legitimate combined behaviors of a project scaffolding tool.

high_findings.title: External command execution via subprocess
high_findings.description: subprocess.run(['pip', 'freeze']) called at line 884 to capture installed package versions for reproducibility documentation. Arguments are hardcoded, no user input injection vector exists. Legitimate use for environment capture.

high_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm references in documentation
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged md5sum and similar references as weak cryptographic algorithms. All instances are in documentation examples showing reproducibility verification steps (checksum comparison), not in actual cryptographic code. Dismissed as false positive.

high_findings.title: Recursive delete pattern in Dockerfile template
high_findings.description: rm -rf appears inside a Dockerfile template string at line 1163. This is 'rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*' which is a standard Docker best practice to clean apt cache after package install. No actual recursive deletion of root or home directories occurs. Dismissed as false positive.

high_findings.title: Heuristic: dangerous combination of code execution, network, and credential access patterns
high_findings.description: Static analyzer detected combined patterns suggesting malicious intent. Evaluation found: subprocess runs pip freeze only (hardcoded args), network references are template badge/license URLs, and no credential access occurs (PYTHONHASHSEED is set, not read). Dismissed as false positive - this is legitimate project scaffolding functionality.

medium_findings.title: Shell command patterns in SKILL.md documentation
medium_findings.description: Ruby/shell backtick execution patterns detected in SKILL.md. All instances are inside fenced code blocks showing example bash commands for users (pip install, conda, pytest). These are documentation examples, not executable code. Dismissed as false positive.

medium_findings.title: File system write operations
medium_findings.description: The tool writes generated project files (source code, tests, config files) to user-specified output directory. This is the core intended functionality of the code generation tool. Writes are scoped to the output directory provided by the user.

medium_findings.title: System hostname collection for reproducibility
medium_findings.description: platform.node() called to capture hostname as part of reproducibility metadata. This is standard practice for documenting the execution environment and the data stays local in a generated JSON file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in template content
low_findings.description: URLs found in template strings for generated README and configuration files. These are badge URLs (shields.io), license URLs (apache.org), ORCID URLs, and placeholder repository URLs. They are template text, not actual HTTP requests.

low_findings.title: Environment variable access for reproducibility
low_findings.description: os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] set at line 812 to ensure deterministic dictionary ordering. This is a write operation for reproducibility purposes, not credential reading.

low_findings.title: Unicode escape in R parser regex
low_findings.description: Unicode escape sequence \u003c used in regex pattern for parsing R function definitions (line 99). This is a legitimate pattern for matching '<-' assignment operator in R code. Dismissed as false positive.

low_findings.title: Hidden file references in template content
low_findings.description: References to hidden files (.cache, .gitkeep, .gitignore) are in GitHub Actions configuration templates and generated project files. These are legitimate template patterns for standard project scaffolding. Dismissed as false positive.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance patterns in audit result JSON
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance flagged in code-refactor-for-reproducibility_audit_result_v2.json. This file is pre-computed static analysis output, not executable code. Dismissed as false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External command execution via subprocess
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run is used to execute 'pip freeze' for capturing environment dependency versions. While the arguments are hardcoded and safe, the pattern of executing external commands within a skill carries inherent risk if modified.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unrestricted file write to user-specified paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The tool creates directories and writes files to user-specified output paths. File paths are constructed programmatically based on template names. No path traversal protection is explicitly implemented.

content.user_title: Transform Research Code into Reproducible Workflows
content.value_statement: Research code often lacks documentation, error handling, and environment specifications needed for journal publication. This skill analyzes messy Python or R scripts and generates a complete, publication-ready project structure compliant with top journal standards.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes Python and R scripts to extract functions, imports, classes, and code structure
Generates complete project structure with src/, tests/, data/, docs/, and results/ directories
Creates publication-ready documentation including README, LICENSE, and CITATION.cff files
Produces environment specification files (environment.yml, requirements.txt) with pinned dependencies
Generates Dockerfile and GitHub Actions CI/CD configuration for automated testing
Implements error handling, logging, and random seed management in all generated source code
limitations: Does not modify original source files - generates a new project structure in a separate output directory
Cannot automatically migrate custom analysis logic into generated modules - manual migration is required
Does not validate that refactored code produces identical results to the original analysis
Limited to Python and R languages only; other languages are not supported

use_cases.title: Prepare Lab Code for Journal Submission
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs to submit analysis code alongside a paper to a Nature or Science journal. This skill analyzes existing scripts and generates a compliant project structure with documentation, tests, and environment files.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researchers preparing code for publication

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Code for Collaboration
use_cases.description: A lab manager wants all team members to follow the same project structure and documentation standards. This skill provides a consistent template for new and existing projects across the research group.

use_cases.title: Improve Personal Research Reproducibility
use_cases.description: A data scientist has accumulated analysis scripts without documentation or environment tracking. This skill analyzes the scripts and creates a structured project with pinned dependencies and reproducibility documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Code Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the research code in /path/to/scripts for reproducibility. Use Python as the language and the nature journal template.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with a single script to refactor

prompt_templates.title: Refactor with Specific Journal Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the R analysis scripts in /home/user/analysis/ for publication. Use the science journal template and name the project rna-seq-analysis. Set the output directory to /home/user/refactored.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher targeting a specific journal format

prompt_templates.title: Batch Refactoring with Custom Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor all Python scripts in /data/experiments/ for reproducibility. Use the elife template. For each subdirectory, create a separate project. Ensure all output projects include Docker configuration and GitHub Actions workflows.
prompt_templates.scenario: Lab manager processing multiple experiment folders

prompt_templates.title: Full Reproducibility Pipeline with Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the code in /research/project/ for Nature journal compliance. Language is Python. After generating the project structure, provide a step-by-step guide for: 1) migrating original functions, 2) filling placeholder metadata, 3) testing reproducibility with fresh environment, 4) archiving on Zenodo for DOI.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user preparing a complete publication package

output_examples.input: Refactor the Python analysis scripts in the folder for reproducibility.
output_examples.output: The skill analyzes 3 Python scripts in the input folder. It detects 12 functions, 8 import statements, and 2 data file references. A new project folder is created with src/, tests/, data/, docs/, and results/ directories. Documentation files include README.md with badges, LICENSE (MIT), and CITATION.cff. Environment files pin numpy 1.24.3 and pandas 2.0.3.

output_examples.input: Refactor the R scripts in the experiment folder for Science journal compliance.
output_examples.output: The skill analyzes 2 R scripts containing 5 functions using base R and ggplot2. A Science-compliant project is generated with comprehensive documentation, Apache-2.0 license, and environment.yml. Generated source code includes data loading, validation, and analysis modules with docstrings and error handling.

output_examples.input: Refactor my research code and add reproducibility testing setup.
output_examples.output: The skill creates the full project structure and adds pytest-based unit tests for data loading and analysis modules. A GitHub Actions workflow for automated testing on Python 3.9, 3.10, and 3.11 is configured. A Dockerfile with Python 3.10-slim is included for containerized execution.

best_practices: Always back up original scripts before running the refactoring tool
Review all generated files and fill in placeholder values such as author names, project descriptions, and ORCID identifiers
Test the refactored pipeline in a fresh virtual environment to verify all dependencies resolve correctly
anti_patterns: Running the tool without reviewing generated placeholders, which can result in incomplete metadata in CITATION.cff and README
Expecting automatic migration of complex analysis logic into generated modules without manual intervention
Ignoring the generated environment files and continuing to use un-pinned dependencies in the refactored project

faq.question: What programming languages does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports Python and R. It analyzes scripts in these languages to extract functions, imports, and structure.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my original source files?
faq.answer: No. The skill reads your original scripts for analysis and generates a new project structure in a separate output directory.

faq.question: What journal templates are available?
faq.answer: Three templates are available: nature (MIT license, comprehensive docs), science (Apache-2.0 license, comprehensive docs), and elife (MIT license, standard docs).

faq.question: Do I need to install additional tools to use the generated project?
faq.answer: The generated project requires Python 3.9+ and standard scientific Python packages. Environment files are provided for both conda and pip.

faq.question: How does the generated code ensure reproducibility?
faq.answer: Generated code includes fixed random seeds, pinned dependency versions, environment specification files, logging, and input validation.

faq.question: Can I customize the generated project structure?
faq.answer: The structure is determined by the selected journal template. After generation you can modify files as needed.
